title: Measuring economic development
summary: This lesson distinguishes between economic growth and the broader concept of economic development. It explores how development is measured using single indicators like GNI per capita and composite indicators like the Human Development Index (HDI), while also critically evaluating their limitations.
learningObjectives: Distinguish between economic growth and economic development.
Explain single indicators of development, including GNI per capita, health, and education statistics.
Explain the components and purpose of the Human Development Index (HDI) as a composite indicator.
Evaluate the strengths and limitations of using various indicators to measure economic development.

simpleExplanation.title: A Nation's Report Card: Beyond the Bank Balance
simpleExplanation.summary: Judging a country's success solely on its income (GDP) is like judging a student on just one test score. Economic development requires a full 'report card' that includes health, education, and living standards to get a true picture of well-being.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Think of a student's school report. A high mark in Maths is great, but it doesn't tell you if they are a well-rounded student. To understand their overall progress, you need to look at their results in English and Science, their attendance record, and perhaps even their participation in sports or clubs. The Human Development Index (HDI) is like that full school report for a country, combining income with health and education to give a more complete and balanced view of its progress.
steps: First, distinguish between economic growth (a rise in real national income) and economic development (a multi-dimensional improvement in living standards, freedom, and well-being).
Next, analyse single indicators used to measure development, such as GNI per capita (PPP$), life expectancy at birth, and mean years of schooling, noting what each reveals and what it omits.$
Then, explain how composite indicators, primarily the Human Development Index (HDI), combine these single measures into one index to provide a more holistic view of a country's development.

Finally, evaluate the limitations of these indicators, considering issues like data accuracy, income inequality, and the omission of key factors like political freedom or environmental sustainability.

flashcards.back: An increase in the real output of an economy over time, typically measured as the percentage change in real GDP or GNI.
A multidimensional concept involving improvements in standards of living, reductions in poverty, and increased freedom and economic choice. It is a qualitative as well as quantitative change.
Gross National Income per person, adjusted for Purchasing Power Parity. It measures the average income of a country's citizens, accounting for differences in the cost of living.
An exchange rate that equates the purchasing power of different currencies by comparing the prices of a common basket of goods and services.
A composite indicator measuring average achievement in three key dimensions of human development: a long and healthy life, being knowledgeable, and having a decent standard of living.

flashcards.front: What are the three dimensions of the HDI?
flashcards.back: 1. A long and healthy life (measured by life expectancy at birth). 2. Knowledge (measured by mean and expected years of schooling). 3. A decent standard of living (measured by GNI per capita, PPP$).$
A specific, measurable variable used to track one aspect of development, such as infant mortality rate or adult literacy rate.
An index created by combining two or more single indicators into a single value, providing a more holistic view. The HDI is a key example.
The average number of years a newborn infant is expected to live if current mortality patterns were to stay the same.
The average number of years of education received by people aged 25 and older. It is a component of the HDI's education dimension.

flashcards.front: Limitation of GNI per capita as a development measure?
flashcards.back: It's an average and hides income inequality. It also doesn't account for unpaid work, informal markets, or negative externalities like pollution.
It does not measure inequality, poverty depth, human security, empowerment, or environmental sustainability.

faq.q: Why is GNI per capita adjusted for Purchasing Power Parity (PPP)?
faq.a: PPP is used to adjust for differences in the cost of living between countries. A certain income, say $1,000, can buy a lot more goods and services in a country with low prices than in one with high prices. The PPP adjustment converts GNI into international dollars with the same purchasing power, allowing for a more accurate comparison of living standards.$

faq.q: If a country has a high HDI, does that mean everyone in the country is well-off?
faq.a: Not necessarily. The HDI is an average and can mask significant inequalities within a country. For example, a country might have a high HDI due to a very wealthy elite and a large, educated urban population, while a rural minority lives in poverty with little access to healthcare or schools. The UN also publishes an Inequality-adjusted HDI (IHDI) to account for this.

faq.q: Are there any alternatives to the HDI?
faq.a: Yes, several alternative and complementary indices exist. The Inequality-adjusted HDI (IHDI) discounts the HDI for inequality. The Gender Inequality Index (GII) measures gender disparities. The Multidimensional Poverty Index (MPI) measures poverty at the individual level across different deprivations. Others, like the Happy Planet Index, try to incorporate environmental sustainability.

sections.content: Imagine two countries. Country A has a very high average income, but a small elite holds most of the wealth, life expectancy is average, and many children leave school early. Country B has a more modest average income, but it is distributed more evenly, its citizens live long lives, and education is universal. Which country is more 'developed'? This lesson explores how economists attempt to answer that question, moving beyond simple income measures to capture the true meaning of human development.
Distinguishing Economic Growth from Economic Development

It is crucial not to use these terms interchangeably. Economic growth refers to the increase in a country's real national output over time. It is a quantitative measure, often expressed as the percentage change in real Gross Domestic Product (GDP) or Gross National Income (GNI). Economic development, however, is a much broader, qualitative concept. It encompasses improvements in a wide range of indicators that affect human well-being, such as health, education, freedom from oppression, and the ability to make choices. While economic growth is often a necessary condition for development, it is not sufficient on its own.

items: **Growth:** Increase in real GNI/GDP. It is a means to an end.
**Development:** Improvement in living standards, freedoms, and well-being. It is the end goal.
A country can have economic growth without significant economic development, for example, if the benefits are not shared or if it comes at a high environmental cost.

sections.content: Measuring Development: Single and Composite Indicators

To measure development, economists use a range of indicators. Single indicators measure one specific dimension. For example, GNI per capita (PPP$) measures average income, life expectancy measures health, and adult literacy rate measures education. While useful, single indicators provide a narrow view. To overcome this, composite indicators were created. These combine several single indicators into a single index to give a more holistic picture of development.$

sections.question: Country X has a Gross National Income (GNI) of $450 billion. Its population is 20 million. Country Y has a GNI of $1.2 trillion ($1,200 billion) and a population of 150 million. Calculate the GNI per capita for both countries and state which country has a higher standard of living based on this single indicator.$
sections.solution:
To calculate GNI per capita, we use the formula: GNI per capita = Total GNI / Population.

**For Country X:**
*   GNI per capita = $450,000,000,000 / 20,000,000$
*   GNI per capita = $22,500$

**For Country Y:**
*   GNI per capita = $1,200,000,000,000 / 150,000,000$
*   GNI per capita = $8,000$

**Conclusion:**
Based solely on the GNI per capita indicator, Country X has a higher average standard of living ($22,500 per person) compared to Country Y ($8,000 per person), despite having a much smaller total GNI.

[**Marking:** 1 mark for the correct formula/method. 1 mark for the correct calculation for Country X. 1 mark for the correct calculation for Country Y. 1 mark for the correct conclusion.]

sections.content: The most widely recognised composite indicator is the United Nations' Human Development Index (HDI). It provides a summary measure of average achievement in key dimensions of human development. The HDI is the geometric mean of normalised indices for each of its three dimensions.

items: **Dimension 1: A long and healthy life.** Measured by life expectancy at birth.
**Dimension 2: Knowledge.** Measured by a combination of mean years of schooling (for adults aged 25+) and expected years of schooling (for children of school-entering age).
**Dimension 3: A decent standard of living.** Measured by GNI per capita, adjusted for purchasing power parity (PPP$).$
The final HDI score ranges from 0 to 1, with countries grouped into four tiers: very high, high, medium, and low human development.

sections.content:
Conceptually, the HDI can be thought of as: 
$HDI = \sqrt[3]{Health \ Index \times Education \ Index \times Income \ Index}$

When asked to evaluate the use of GNI per capita or the HDI to measure development, you must provide a balanced argument. For GNI, mention its simplicity and correlation with other indicators (strength) but also its failure to show inequality or non-marketed output (weakness). For HDI, praise its multidimensional nature (strength) but criticise what it omits, such as inequality (though the IHDI addresses this), political freedom, and environmental damage (weaknesses).

sections.question:
The table below shows 2021 data for Switzerland and Chad. 

| Indicator | Switzerland | Chad |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| HDI Value | 0.962 | 0.394 |
| Life Expectancy | 84.0 years | 52.5 years |
| Expected Years of Schooling | 16.5 years | 8.0 years |
| GNI per capita (PPP$) | $66,933 | $1,472 |$

Using the data, compare and contrast the level of economic development in Switzerland and Chad.
sections.solution:
**Comparison:**
*   Switzerland has a significantly higher level of economic development than Chad, as indicated by its 'very high' HDI value of 0.962 compared to Chad's 'low' HDI value of 0.394. [1 mark]
*   This disparity is reflected across all three components of the HDI. Switzerland's GNI per capita ($66,933) is vastly higher than Chad's ($1,472), indicating a much higher average standard of living. [1 mark]
*   Furthermore, health outcomes are far superior in Switzerland, with a life expectancy of 84.0 years, over 30 years longer than in Chad (52.5 years). [1 mark]
*   Educational attainment and opportunity are also much greater in Switzerland, where a child can expect 16.5 years of schooling, more than double the expectation in Chad (8.0 years). [1 mark]

**Contrast:**
*   The data illustrates the stark contrast between a highly developed nation and a least developed one. Switzerland excels in all three dimensions, indicating a population that is, on average, wealthy, healthy, and well-educated. 
*   In contrast, Chad faces severe development challenges across all three dimensions, with low income, poor health outcomes, and limited access to education. The data shows that development is not just about income, but a combination of factors that are all interlinked.

[**Marking:** Marks are awarded for making explicit data-driven comparisons for each component of the HDI and for a concluding statement that synthesises the information.]
